speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four with Joseph Brod on March 15th, 1996 in San Diego, California.

speaker: interviewee
text: One thing I would like to bring out, even though in much of the literature, I didn't see much reference to it. It was one of the dirtiest tricks of the Nazis. They had a council of the elders of the Jews, and those people in the council had to nominate the people who should be sent to the camps, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] otherwise, they would land in the camps themselves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was one of the dirtiest tricks I've ever seen. Do I make myself clear enough?

speaker: interviewer
text: What exactly did they do in Vienna then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they, for example, they had the rest of the Jews, whom-- who-- we need now 30, let's say, 30 or 40. Whom do you nominate? They had to nominate, or they would go themselves. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your opinion about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was one of the pieces of dirtiest footwork that you could imagine, because those people were decent people otherwise. They certainly didn't love to do that because they were fully aware of what they were doing. It was the Jewish Elder Council. They had that in every city where there were more Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that how you were picked out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was-- mine was through this one fellow, young Nazi who lived in the neighborhood. But many of them, especially later on, those that were deported, they had to nominate those Jews who were deported. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it sounds like you don't blame the people on that council.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't because it was a question who survives? That way they still had a little hope, maybe they would survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you suppose you would have done if you had been on that council?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, and I don't want to judge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tough question.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to be very frank about it. I don't know what I would have done under similar circumstances. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I think it's important for-- for the record that it is stated unmistakably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Well, continuing with your-- your new life here-- another thing you mentioned to me the other day is it you couldn't work with Jewish firms. I wasn't sure what you meant there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I will tell you why. You see, and this is not-- in any respect, let's call it anti-Semitic. I want to you to understand it. But the culture and the upbringing of the Jews who came from the East, were so different by necessity because, for them, it was strictly survival. So they were forced into all kinds of tricks to survive. But in middle Europe, that didn't apply anymore, because in this connection, the Jews were like all the others. They didn't get away with it. And when they started to play the same tricks for survival, this created a lot of hatred and [? distrust. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a pity because those people were victims of their own background. I'm not blaming them. I might have done the same thing under similar circumstances. But that created-- not created, but contributed to the anti-Semitism. I know-- I knew quite a few people who later on were Nazis. They said to me, you Viennese Jews, you don't have to worry. Nothing will happen to you-- only the Eastern Jews. I don't-- don't kid me, I said. You will start with them and you will finish with us. I was outspoken about those things. I didn't care. And but actually, the joke of it was those, because they were very often still citizens of their old countries, they were better protected for a while than we were. That's why, for example, Dachau, there was mainly Austrian and German Jews, no Eastern Jews at that time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Along the way, you mentioned a humorous definition of a Zionist the other day--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --talking about Jewish character in Israel and so on. Could you explain that a little?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want me to repeat it?

speaker: interviewer
text: We could use a joke here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it's a sad joke. That was a definition that they had there. A Zionist is a Jew who collects money from other Jews to send other Jews to Israel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there was a good deal of truism that it was a subconscious thing. Look, here many people call themselves Zionists. If I were a Zionist, I would liquidate the little bit I have. I would move to Israel tomorrow, regardless of the circumstance. That it is what, in my opinion, a Zionist should be. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How come you didn't go there yourself? Did you have that option?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't have the option, as I told you before. When I came out of the camp, I went to the Jewish community, and there was this delegate from the Zionist organization. I said, could you get me on the transport? I have to leave here within three, four weeks. He said, no, you have to go to [INAUDIBLE], to Western Germany for six months. What would have happened to me there? And he knew it. It wasn't that he didn't know, regardless, because I wasn't a Zionist.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, Zionism didn't take strength really, except among some of the young Jews in the middle Europe at all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were Jewish by religion and by definition. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But basically they were products of the culture of the country where they were. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The only time the Jews got together, for example, in Vienna, the better situated ones, was once a year to a big representative ball. You know what I mean by ball? Big affair with dance and so. Otherwise, there was little contact. Well, a similar theme you mentioned the other day, the line was that you have to be taught to hate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you explain what you meant by that in terms of the anti-Semitic culture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, you know, well, I forgot his name now. A Frenchman was one of the spiritual fathers of anti-Semitism. He wrote in French a book about the inequality of the human races. A nobleman-- I-- I knew the name perfectly, and I can't remember it. Even somewhere I have the book, I think. And that actually started-- maybe this book started a movement. There was a sense-- a piece of sand that started the desert. Because then, in-- in Europe, middle Europe, especially Germany, Austria, until then, the Jews were ghetto Jews during this-- in the 1700s.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Moses Mendelssohn in Germany took hand and taught them German. Not-- it was not Yiddish. They didn't speak Yiddish. It was so-called Juden Deutch, Jewish German, that distinguished them from the other Germans, and the Germans never liked it because it was to them like a distortion, like a parody. So he taught the Jews correct German, high German. And that's what opened basically, spiritually, the ghetto doors. They were never 100% equal but they were more equal, you see? But when you said that people have to be taught to hate, what was that implying about--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was here. You had then the antagonism. All of a sudden, you had a class who wanted equal rights, or nearly equal rights. But there were people who were not interested in having them equal rights so they answered it with hate and distortion. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because normally, especially in the Middle Ages-- and I don't call it anti-Semism-- anti-Judaism, because it had nothing to do with Semites. That was a natural background. Look, even Goethe, until his very latest years, he didn't think anything of the Jews, and such a leading man, because they had different, until then, in their way of speaking and behaving and clothing. And then, when they became nearly equal, then there were people who didn't like it because they didn't have anybody to step on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The other day I think your implication was that the people around you weren't necessarily anti-Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, most of them were not.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that-- but that from the intelligentsia, they had to be taught.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not only that, but there were a lot of intermarriages. I know, for example, in the building-- apartment building next to us, there was a Sephardi Jew, a very fine gentleman, and he was married to a Catholic. And the sister of this woman worked in the same firm that I worked. And then when the Nazis came into power, they arrested him. She was all upset. [INAUDIBLE], she said, one of the finest men I've ever known, and they take him away because he's a Jew. There was a lot of internal resistance among the Viennese Jews, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but they didn't dare to show it. They still visited us when they thought that they wouldn't be discovered. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, moving up closer to the present, how do you feel about surviving, as you did, when so many of the people you left behind didn't make it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Will you repeat it, so I that I [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about surviving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Survival of what?

speaker: interviewer
text: The war, the camps, all of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the next war, there won't be much survival. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I never believed in this so-called [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] millennium. You know the millennium in Catholic church is a very bad affair-- destruction. So I think, good. Now, we are coming to the year 2000. I always shrug my shoulders at it. But there seems to be something in the atmosphere that does it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's in the people's minds. It's not that it is automatically-- automatically by history a force. It's in the people's subconscious minds. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So surviving the Holocaust wasn't that significant to you because we're all gonna get wiped out soon?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was significant for me as a person, but only as a person.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, how? In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I was still alive, that I could move, that I could do things. I could marry the girl I loved, as a person, strictly as a person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of all the Jews in Europe, how come you were one of the minority who survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gentleman above knows more about it than I do. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Does it mean anything to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, it means something to-- there is a-- do you understand some German?

speaker: interviewer
text: You can translate.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will try to translate it, but it doesn't sound so good. God's ways are incomprehensible to us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's why, for example, if you take the Kabbalah, God is the unknowable God. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We don't know. He knows, but we don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, with all of your knowledge, obviously, of Jewish history and there's a million books here that we're not showing on [INTERPOSING VOICES] the camera, you're quite a scholar. What is your understanding of how the Holocaust fits into Jewish history or theology or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Look--

speaker: interviewer
text: --what-- what should it mean to us? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you what, in my opinion, it means to us. It depends on what the others do to us. We have very little influence on it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We are not asked. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we can only cross our fingers and hope for the best, that gradually maybe they become more human-- humane, not human, humane. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Look, even conversion, actually, in Europe the conversion, you didn't count as a Christian until in the third generation. Until then, you were a baptized Jew. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And you were looked down upon from both sides. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You were actually, in a way, a social misfit. You might have had money, but you still were a social misfit [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because you were neither, should I say, neither the one thing or the other. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were saying how that the Jewish people don't have much control over this, it's how other people--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right--

speaker: interviewer
text: --what other people do, what message would you want to-- to say to, well, all the others? That's a big--

speaker: interviewee
text: Look--

speaker: interviewer
text: --generality. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I could only come back to you have to be taught to hate. That means you can be taught also not to hate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what should people be taught about their attitude towards Jewishness? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: That, to a certain extent, it's partly, especially here in the States, a segregation of the Jews from the non-Jews, which didn't exist there. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Because here, for example, when-- in Jewish circles, when they talk about the goy, they think the other doesn't understand. They know exactly what goy is, and they don't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so maybe to turn the question around, what should the Jewish people learn from the Holocaust? Should anything be changed in values or attitudes or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you the only change cannot come from the Jews, only from the others. It can be done, but it will take centuries, at least three, four generations. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because whatever we do, it's wrong. Let's face the fact. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- there was-- you-- you heard about Lessing, the German writer and philosopher? He wrote about Jewish faith-- Nathan, the wise one. And that place still in the Palestine, and around the Crusade time when the Christians still has a lot of influence on the Mohammedans. And there was a Jew, a wealthy Jew, there whom the sultan liked very much, but the Catholic bishop didn't like it. And because one girl that he brought up was Christian, and he brought her up as a Christian, the-- the bishop wanted the Jew should be burned. And there is this argument that Lessing brings up for the Jews, because the bishop says, it doesn't matter. The Jews should be burned.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a basic psychology that we have to fight. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it's a tough fight. And every so often, we progress, progress. Then there is a throwback.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're gonna to look at your family photographs in a moment. Is there anything else you'd like to add before we do that, any final thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you ask me what. I want to answer your questions because you know much better what you want to ask.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's look at the pictures, and we'll see how much time we have left after that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll tell you when. OK, Joseph, tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken about 1960. On the left, this is my wife Martha. Her maiden name was Picker-- P-I-C-K-E-R. In the middle, that's my mother-in-law, Lena Picker. And on the right is the older sister of my wife, Sarah Picker.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken, as I said, about 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? And with a very tiny camera. That's why it's not a very good picture. It was one of the spy cameras.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, stand by and tell us about this picture, Joseph.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my father. It was taken about-- I can tell pretty exact. Mother died in August 31st, '38, '38, and that was taken shortly afterwards. That's why he has the ribbon there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna. And where we lived in Vienna, in the 18th district.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not '50s, late '20s, I mean, pardon me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead and tell us about this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Mathilde-- M-A-T-H-I-L-D-E, and her maiden name was Vohryzek. I have to spell it?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's written down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And she was, at that time, probably in their 50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Carlsbad. She was visiting with her sister there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go getting up to speed, and go ahead, Joseph.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead and tell us about this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Start--

speaker: interviewee
text: He died when he was 83. He worked till the last day of his life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Martha's father, my father-in-law. Wonderful person.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell-- Tell us where it was taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us where it was taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where it was taken? In Chicago. He was an old Chicago institution.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead, Joseph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, so to say, our wedding picture. It was taken shortly after our wedding. It was taken in September. It was September '40. We were married August, '40.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Martha, and her maiden name was Picker-- P-I-C-K-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wanted to talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead and tell us about this one.

words.text: and tell us about this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: What should I tell you there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell who's in the picture-- this stuff right here.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the picture, it's me and my wife, Martha. And that was taken in the I would say late '80s by a friend of ours, a-- a-- semi-professional. When [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, Joseph. Tell us about the flyer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was in Chicago, when I was part owner with Martha. We owned a camera store in the downtown. And in the early '50s, I contacted the Chamber of Commerce, and they elected me to their education committee. We're, naturally, honorary. And I was there from the '50s until we sold our store in '65. And somehow or other, first time I heard from the organization was the They published me. And when we moved, they republished in Then they republished it in And then there was this one here, and after that came I have the-- the books just standing right up there. How I got into it, I don't know. I didn't look for it. They came to me. But naturally, I didn't refuse it.
